turns.text: Hello Basilia, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're in the middle of your pregnancy, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

Thanks for asking. It’s… it’s a lot to put into words, honestly. I’m twenty-one, and in a place like Holden, that’s young to be doing this on your own. Most days, I feel like I’m walking around with a secret, even though the bump is getting harder to hide.

I spend so much time reading up on fetal development. I know exactly how big the baby is each week—the size of a peach, then an avocado—but knowing those facts doesn't stop the quiet from settling in when I get home. My appointments are fine, really. The midwife is professional, and she’s always checking the boxes: blood pressure, heart rate, weight. It’s always "perfect." But sometimes I want to scream because "perfect" clinical numbers don't account for the fact that I’m terrified of the delivery room. I’m scared that when the time comes, it’ll just be me and the monitors, and that silence is going to feel suffocating.

I’m trying to stay focused on the flutters. They’re getting stronger, and that’s the one time I don’t feel like just a data point in a chart. It’s like a little secret conversation between the two of us. I just wish the people caring for me saw that I’m a person, not just a successful low-risk case.
Tell me about the moment you found out you were pregnant — and what the weeks that followed were like for you.

I remember staring at that little plastic stick in my bathroom, and the world just felt like it stopped moving. It wasn’t a cinematic moment with music; it was just me, the humming of the refrigerator in the other room, and this sudden, cold realization that my entire life had pivoted on a dime. I think I sat on the edge of the tub for an hour, just breathing.

The weeks that followed were… blurred. At first, I was so sick, physically, that I could barely keep water down, which made keeping my job search together feel like an impossible mountain to climb. But the mental part was heavier. I didn’t tell anyone for a while. I kept going to the grocery store, buying milk and bread, acting like I wasn’t carrying this massive, life-altering weight. I felt like I had to be perfect and prepared, so I started buying books and researching everything, thinking that if I knew enough about gestational weeks and nutrition, I could outsmart the fear.

But you can’t research your way out of being alone. I’d be in bed at night, feeling these waves of exhaustion and nausea, and I’d just reach over to the empty side of the bed and cry. It wasn’t just about the morning sickness—it was the realization that I was stepping into this huge, scary unknown without a hand to hold. That’s when the silence first started to feel really loud.
What emotions came up for you — and did those feelings surprise you at all?

It’s strange, because I expected to feel just fear, but it was so much more tangled than that. There was definitely fear—a sharp, cold kind that prickles at the back of your neck when you’re grocery shopping and realize you’re suddenly responsible for two lives instead of one. But there was also this fierce, protective guilt. I felt guilty for being scared, like if I wasn’t 100% joyful every second, I wasn't being a "good" mother. 

The most surprising thing was how lonely the *happiness* felt. When I felt that very first flutter, I gasped, and my eyes filled with tears, and I had this overwhelming urge to turn around and share it with someone—to see someone else’s face light up with that same wonder. When I realized there was no one to turn to, the joy sort of soured into a heavy, aching weight. I didn't think I’d feel that kind of isolation so deeply. I thought I was strong enough to do this as an island, but I’m learning that even if I can carry the baby, I’m not sure I was meant to carry the entire experience of becoming a mother in a vacuum. It makes me feel smaller than I actually am, if that makes sense.
Tell me about your antenatal care — the appointments, check-ups, and the people who looked after you during your pregnancy. What was that experience like overall?

The appointments are efficient, I’ll give them that. I go into the clinic in Holden, wait in that quiet lobby with the older couples, and then I’m ushered back to a room that smells like antiseptic and starch. The midwife is kind enough, I suppose. She’s very thorough with the clinical side—the measuring, the listening, the blood pressure cuff. 

But it’s a very... transactional kind of care. It’s "How are you feeling?" followed by "Good, everything looks perfect," and then the door closes again. I feel like I’m sitting there with a giant neon sign over my head that says *Single, 21, Doing This Solo*, but nobody ever asks about that part. They look at the chart, they look at my belly, and they see a successful, low-risk pregnancy. They don’t see that I’m trying to figure out how to be a parent while I’m still figuring out how to be an adult.

I want to ask them, "What happens when I’m in labor? Who helps me navigate the panic if the machines start beeping?" but I hold my tongue. I don’t want to be "the difficult patient" or the girl who can’t handle it. So, I keep my questions to the baby’s growth, like a good student. It’s lonely, feeling like I’m navigating the most significant event of my life while being treated like a routine check-list.
Did you see the same midwife or provider regularly, or was it different people each time? How did that feel?

It’s been a mix, honestly, and that’s been one of the hardest parts. I’ve seen three different providers so far. Every time I walk in, I have to re-introduce myself and my situation—or worse, I don't, and I just let them read the file like I’m a stranger. 

It feels like I’m constantly starting from square one. Every time I get a new person, I have to gauge their vibe—decide if this is someone who will talk to me like a human or just someone who wants to check my fundal height and get me out the door in fifteen minutes. When you’re doing this alone, you crave a little bit of consistency. You want someone who remembers that you’re worried about the birth plan, or someone who remembers that you didn’t have a partner with you last time. 

Instead, it’s like I’m a recurring guest character in a show where the lead doctors keep changing. There’s no rapport, no "Oh, how are you feeling today, Basilia?" It’s just, "Hi, I’m [Name], let’s see how the baby’s doing." I know they have a lot of patients, and I know I’m "low risk," but that lack of continuity makes the whole process feel so cold. It just reinforces that I’m on my own in this. Nobody really *knows* me; they just know the pregnancy.
Tell me about any screening or testing you were offered or had during your pregnancy — how did you make sense of it all and make decisions about it?

The screening process felt like walking through a minefield of data. When they offered the anatomy scan and the blood work for genetic screening, the information was just dumped on me—all these percentages and probabilities about chromosomal issues. It’s a lot to process when you’re sitting there by yourself. 

I spent nights obsessing over the literature. I went down rabbit holes online because I didn’t have anyone to say, "Hey, take a breath, everything is going to be fine." I made the decisions based mostly on my need to feel prepared. I figured if I knew what I was facing, I could plan for it. I opted for all the screenings because the idea of a surprise—even a medical one—felt scarier than the test results themselves. 

But sitting in the ultrasound room for the anatomy scan was probably the most surreal moment. Watching the screen, seeing the little heart chambers beating, and seeing the tiny hands move... it was beautiful, but it was also another reminder of the silence. I was the only one in the room watching that screen, trying to understand what the technician was seeing. I had to be my own advocate and my own comfort, which is a heavy burden when you’re just trying to make sure your baby is healthy. I don’t think I really "made sense" of it so much as I just survived the anxiety of it.
How was the information about tests explained to you — did you feel you had enough to make the decision that was right for you?

The information was presented like a menu at a restaurant—just a list of options with a brief, clinical explanation of what they looked for. "We recommend this, this, and this," they’d say, pointing to the paperwork. It felt like they expected me to just check the boxes and move on. There wasn’t really a conversation about what those results might actually mean for my life, or how I’d handle the potential outcomes. 

I don't think they really understood the *weight* of the decision for someone in my position. If a result came back indicating a complication, I wouldn't have a partner to process that news with, or to help me weigh the next steps. I felt like I had to be the professional, the researcher, and the patient all at once. I’d ask questions, but the answers were always, "Well, it’s entirely your choice," which feels less like "empowerment" and more like "we’re not going to help you decide." 

I had enough data, sure, but I didn't feel like I had enough *support*. I was making these huge life decisions in a clinical vacuum. I felt like I had to choose the tests that gave me the most certainty, because I couldn't afford to have any questions left unanswered. I just wish someone had sat with me and asked, "How are you feeling about all this?" instead of just waiting for me to sign the forms.
Tell me about where you turned for information and support during your pregnancy — what sources mattered most to you, and why?

Mostly, I turn to the internet, but I’m careful about where I look. I stick to the sites that sound clinical—the ones that talk about fetal lung maturity or the way the brain develops week-by-week. It’s my way of feeling like I’m “studying” for this. If I know exactly what’s happening inside me, I feel less like a vessel and more like a participant. It’s a sad kind of comfort, I know, but it’s the only one I have.

I don’t really have a “village.” My friends are all still in that phase of figuring out what they’re doing on a Friday night, not what kind of car seat is safest for a newborn. I’ve tried mentioning my fears to a couple of them, but they just look at me with this pity that makes me want to shrink away. So, I keep it to myself. 

I’ve been reading these birth stories online—the ones written by other women. I search for the ones that mention being alone or the experience of single motherhood. Sometimes I find a thread where someone talks about how the nurses or the midwife held their hand when they were scared, and I cling to those moments. I save them like instructions. I guess I’m just trying to piece together a version of motherhood from bits and scraps of other people’s experiences because I don’t have an example of it in my own life right now.
Did you use any apps, websites, forums, or social media groups? What did you get from those that you couldn't get elsewhere?

I use a few different apps that track the baby's size—you know, the standard ones. They’re helpful in a mechanical way, but they’re also kind of painful. Every time they send a notification saying, "Your partner can help by doing X, Y, or Z," I just swipe it away as fast as I can. It’s a constant, digital reminder of what’s missing.

I do spend time on some pregnancy forums, but I’m a ghost there. I read the threads, especially the ones about "single mom" experiences. I don't post, though. I’m afraid of being judged, or of getting that same pitying tone I get in real life. What I get from those forums—that I don't get in my actual appointments—is a sense of reality. I see women talking about the physical exhaustion, the fear of the financial weight, and the way the Braxton Hicks contractions feel when you’re home alone at 3:00 AM. 

There’s a comfort in knowing I’m not the only one sitting in the dark, feeling the baby kick, and wondering how on earth I’m going to be a good mother. It’s validation. In the doctor’s office, I feel like I’m supposed to be this perfect, low-risk patient. Online, I can see that I’m allowed to be scared, overwhelmed, and unsure. It’s the only place where the "perfect" facade is allowed to crack.
Tell me about how your body changing during pregnancy felt for you — physically and emotionally. How did you relate to those changes?

It’s a strange, surreal tug-of-war. Physically, I feel like I’m being reclaimed by something else. My clothes don't fit, my center of gravity is off, and my skin feels different. There’s a constant, low-level wonder at the fact that I’m growing a human being—like, actually building a heart and lungs inside of me. When I feel that sharp kick against my ribs, it’s the most incredible thing I’ve ever experienced. It makes me feel capable, almost like I have a superpower.

But the emotional side is a constant ache. I look in the mirror and I see the changes, but I don’t have anyone else to share that reflection with. I remember trying on maternity clothes for the first time, thinking, "This is it, I’m really doing this," and just standing in the dressing room crying because I wanted someone to look at me and say I looked beautiful. 

There’s this vulnerability to it, too. My body is no longer entirely my own, and it feels like it’s on display to these medical professionals who only care about the clinical outcome. They touch me to check the baby’s position, but it feels so detached. I think I relate to my body as a project I’m managing—I have to keep it healthy, keep it fed, and keep it safe—because if I lose control of my body, I lose control of the only thing I have right now.
Tell me about the role your partner, family, or close friends played during your pregnancy. What was their involvement like?

To be honest, the answer is mostly silence. I’m doing this in a bit of a vacuum. My family knows, of course, but there’s a distance there—maybe it’s because I’m twenty-one and still trying to prove I’m an adult, or maybe they just don't know how to reach out to a situation that feels so complicated. It’s not that they aren’t there, but they aren't *here*. They aren't going to the appointments with me, and they aren't sitting on the couch while I navigate the anxiety of a sleepless night. 

As for friends, the gap between us has just widened. We’re in completely different universes now. I can’t talk to them about the reality of a changing body or the terrifying, looming deadline of the delivery date because they’re talking about internships and weekend plans. Trying to explain the weight of this just makes me feel like a burden, or like I’m bringing a "heavy" vibe to the group.

It’s just me. I’ve become very good at being my own support system, but it’s an exhausting job. I find myself wishing for someone to just ask, "How are *you* doing today?" instead of asking about the baby. It’s hard to build a "soft, stable life" when the foundation feels like it’s being built by just one set of hands.
How included did your partner or support person feel in appointments and decisions — and how did you feel about that?

Since I don’t have a partner, the "support person" slot is permanently empty. When I walk into those appointments, the room is designed for a duo. There’s that extra chair in the corner, staring back at me, usually occupied by my bag or a stack of brochures I’ve already read. It’s a very loud kind of silence.

The providers usually don’t even ask if I have someone. They just kind of gloss over it, which I think is their way of being polite—avoiding the awkwardness of my situation. But it makes me feel like I’m an anomaly, or like there’s some piece of the puzzle that I’m supposed to have but somehow lost. 

Sometimes, when the midwife asks, "Do you have any questions about the birth plan?" I want to ask her how I’m supposed to manage the labor room alone. But I don’t. I just nod, because I don't want the pity, and I don't want them to think I'm "struggling." I’ve had to learn to be my own support person, my own decision-maker, and my own cheerleader. It’s heavy, being the only one holding the weight of these choices. I think if there was someone else there, the burden might be split, and I could actually breathe for a second. Instead, it’s just me, the chair, and the medical charts.
Tell me about any worries or fears you carried during your pregnancy — about your health, your baby, the birth, or anything else.

The fears are like shadows; they grow as the day goes on. Most of the time, they’re about the delivery room. I have this recurring, vivid image of the moment I go into labor—the quiet of the house, the long drive to the hospital, and then that final, isolating transition into the maternity ward where everyone else has a partner to lean on. I’m scared that in the middle of that pain, I’ll reach out and realize I’m completely unanchored. I worry that if I lose my nerve, there won't be anyone to ground me.

Then there’s the financial, "real-life" fear. I’m twenty-one, and I’m constantly doing mental math—diapers, formula, pediatrician visits—against whatever I can pull together from my job. It’s like I’m building a house of cards, and I’m terrified that one sudden gust of reality is going to knock it all down. 

And, of course, the baby’s health. Every little twinge or lack of movement makes my heart stop. I know the clinical guidelines for "normal" activity, but when you're alone, you don't trust your own brain. I just want to be able to say, "Hey, does this feel okay?" to someone who actually cares about the answer. I’m terrified that I’ll miss something, or that I won't be enough, and it’ll be entirely my fault.
Were you able to share those fears with your care team — and if so, how did they respond?

I’ve tried, once or twice. I remember being at a check-up a few weeks ago—my heart was racing, and I just blurted out that I was terrified about the birth, about being alone for the delivery. I expected maybe a little reassurance, or even just a moment where the midwife would pause what she was doing and look me in the eye. 

Instead, she just kept on with the chart, writing something down, and said, "It’s very common to have anxiety, Basilia. You’re doing great, and everything is looking perfectly healthy." It felt like she was reading from a script. She checked off the box for "mental health screening" or whatever, and that was it. She didn't ask *what* specifically scared me, or what it felt like to be doing this on my own. 

It taught me pretty quickly that I shouldn't bring my internal world into the clinic. They’re there to look at the anatomy, the blood work, and the fetal position. My fears? Those are "background noise." If I tell them I’m scared, they just offer me a brochure for a support group I don’t have time to go to, or they tell me I’m "low risk," which sounds like a dismissal. So, I learned to keep the fear inside. I pack it away with my insurance card, and I present the version of myself that they expect: the quiet, low-maintenance patient who doesn't have any complications. It’s safer that way.

When you first started thinking about having a child, what did you hope your relationship with your healthcare providers would look like — and were there any ways you worried it might not live up to that?

I think I pictured something like a partnership. You see it in movies or read it in books—that mentor figure, someone who walks through the fire with you. I imagined my midwife being the person who could calm my racing heart when the ultrasound was too much, someone who would recognize that being twenty-one and alone isn’t just a "low-risk" box to check—it’s a life-altering, scary reality. I wanted someone I could trust with the messier parts of my brain, not just the clinical ones.

I worried pretty early on that I’d be viewed as "that girl." You know, the young, single mom who is just a statistic. I feared that because I’m so young, they wouldn't take my questions seriously, or that they’d be patronizing and talk to me like I didn’t know what I was reading or what my body was doing. 

The reality is colder than I feared. It’s not that they’re *mean*—they’re professional—but it’s that distance. It’s like they’re doctors, and I’m just a vessel with a baby inside. I realized that if I want to be "seen," I have to force them to look at me, and honestly? I’m too tired for that. So I just settle for being a "successful case." It’s safer, even if it feels hollow.

Thinking back to when you and your partner — or the people closest to you — were weighing up whether and when to start trying for a baby, how did those conversations actually go? Who felt like they had the most say, and did that feel right to you?

That conversation didn’t happen like it does for those older couples I see in the waiting room—the ones who sit there with their schedules and their "five-year plans." My situation was never about a conscious, joint decision to "start trying." It was a sudden, earth-shaking reality that I was already *in* it before I even had the chance to sit down and debate the logistics.

There wasn't a "we" to weigh the pros and cons. When I found out, the person who was supposed to be the "we" just... evaporated. The lack of a partner meant I didn't have anyone to share that weight with, but it also meant I didn't have to fight anyone for a say, either. I suppose, in a dark, twisted way, I had all the say. Every choice from here on out—the prenatal vitamins, the doctor I picked, the nursery setup—is mine. 

It feels right in the sense that I’m responsible for this little life, and I take that duty seriously. But it feels deeply, profoundly *wrong* because I’m the only one standing here. I’m twenty-one, and I have to be both the mother and the person who decides if we can afford the expensive diapers or the slightly cheaper ones. I don’t want all the say; I want to be able to talk to someone who cares about the result as much as I do. Being the sole decision-maker isn’t power—it’s just being solitary.
Did older family members — parents or grandparents — have a view that found its way into the decision, even indirectly?

Oh, they had a view, alright. Even if they didn’t say it directly to my face, it hung in the air like a thick, heavy fog. I think for my parents, there’s this specific, quiet kind of disappointment—not necessarily in me, but in the situation. They’re from a generation where things were supposed to happen in a specific order: marriage first, then the house, then the baby. Being twenty-one and never married is... it’s not the plan they had for me.

Sometimes I see it in the way my mom looks at me when I’m struggling to get off the sofa, or how my dad asks, "How are you going to manage the finances?" with that tight-lipped concern. It’s not malicious, but it’s heavy. It’s like their worry is another layer of fear I have to climb over every single day. I feel like I’m constantly trying to prove to them that I’m not just a "mistake" or a tragedy waiting to happen. 

I’ve had to build a wall around my pregnancy to keep their expectations from smothering me. If I let their anxiety in, I’d never get out of bed. So, I keep it professional with them, too. "Everything is fine," I tell them. "The doctor said the baby is healthy." I give them the data points so they’ll stop looking at me with that hurt in their eyes. It’s exhausting, honestly—trying to keep everyone calm while I’m the one actually living through the storm.
Before you became pregnant, how did you feel about your body and what it was capable of — and did those feelings change at all as you started thinking about pregnancy?

Before all this, I related to my body as an object. It was something to keep fit, to dress, to show up for work in. It was a tool, really—something I needed to keep functioning so I could be independent. I was proud of it, I guess, in a detached way. It was mine, and I had total control over it.

When I got pregnant, that sense of ownership just... evaporated. The morning sickness in the beginning felt like a betrayal. I was so used to having my own rhythm, and suddenly I was at the mercy of hormones, fatigue, and hunger. It was like I was a tenant in my own skin, and the landlord was this tiny, demanding stranger.

Now, though, my relationship to it has shifted into something both more intense and more fearful. When I feel the baby move, I am in absolute awe—I’m struck by the fact that my body is doing something that feels like magic. But the fear is always right there, too. I worry that my body isn't "strong enough" or "resilient enough" to carry this to term without someone to lean on. I look at my stomach and wonder if it’s capable of the labor ahead, or if I’m just waiting for it to fail me when the pressure gets too high. It’s not just a body anymore; it’s a vessel, and I’m terrified of dropping it.
Were there things about your body you felt confident about, and things that worried you or felt uncertain?

I’ve always been confident in my body’s ability to just *work*. Like, I’ve always been a hard worker, someone who could pull a long shift, go to school, and not fall apart. I liked that efficiency. It felt like I was the captain of the ship. But now, that confidence is fading because "working hard" doesn't necessarily mean "making a baby."

I’m terrified that I’m somehow doing it wrong. I worry that my stress levels—which are pretty constant, living like this—are actually damaging the baby's development. I read about how cortisol can affect a fetus, and then I spiral. I start wondering if my body is reflecting my life: tired, anxious, and stretched too thin. 

The most uncertain part is the actual, physical act of birth. I’ve read all the clinical descriptions, I know the stages, I know what happens when. But the reality? That scares me to death. I don’t know if my body is going to know what to do when there’s no partner to tell me to breathe or squeeze a hand. I feel like I’m waiting for a test I haven’t studied for, and the stakes couldn't be higher. I want to trust my instincts, but I’m too busy trying to keep up with the medical checklists to actually listen to what my body might be trying to tell me. It’s a lot to navigate when you feel like the ground is shifting beneath your feet.

If you saw a GP, nurse, or any other health professional before you became pregnant — even just for something routine — what was that experience like, and did it give you any sense of what maternity care might be like down the line?

Before I got pregnant, I mostly saw my primary care doctor for the standard stuff—yearly physicals, the occasional check-up for a cold or a prescription refill. It was always very quick, very "get in, get out." I’d sit on that exam table in the paper gown, listening to the muffled sounds of the office staff laughing in the hallway, and I’d just be another name on their list for the day.

That experience actually gave me a pretty good—and honestly, pretty depressing—idea of what maternity care would feel like. I remember thinking, "If this is how they handle a sinus infection, how are they going to handle a human life?" It felt so cold, so mechanical. I’d be in and out in ten minutes, and the doctor would barely make eye contact because they were already looking at their computer screen or the next patient's file.

I think I carried that expectation into this pregnancy. I knew I wasn't going to get a "soulful" experience; I knew it was going to be about charts and clinical boxes. It’s part of why I feel so alone in all of this. I’m just a patient to them, and they are just service providers to me. There’s no space for a human connection, and I’ve been conditioned to think that’s just how healthcare works. It’s efficient, sure, but it’s devastatingly hollow when you’re facing something this big.
Before you were pregnant, what kinds of conversations were you having with the people around you — family, friends, your partner — about having a baby, and whose input felt most meaningful to you?

Before I got pregnant, the topic of "having a baby" was almost theoretical. It was something my friends would giggle about—like, "Can you imagine having a kid right now? We’d never have any fun again!" My parents would make those offhand comments about how I should focus on my education and career, maybe get settled before thinking about "the next stage." It was all very abstract, a world away from where I was actually living.

My partner—or who I *thought* was my partner—would occasionally talk about it in the same abstract, playful way. "Someday," he’d say, and it felt like a nice, distant horizon. Looking back, those conversations were incredibly shallow. We were talking about a life-altering event like it was just another vacation we might take in a few years. It wasn’t a real discussion about values, or fear, or what it actually means to build a life.

Because it was all so hypothetical, no one's input felt truly "meaningful" in the way I need now. I wish someone had talked to me about the reality of the weight. Instead, everyone was just playing with ideas. Now that I’m here, the gravity of it feels so disconnected from those lighthearted, "someday" chats. It makes me feel like I walked into a hurricane without even knowing it was raining.

Were there things about your own body, your health history, or just the way you felt about yourself physically that made the idea of pregnancy feel complicated or uncertain — even before anything had actually happened?

I think I’ve always been hyper-aware of my body as something that needed to be "managed." My parents were always pushing me to be independent—don't rely on anyone, keep your grades up, keep your job, keep moving. I took that to heart. My body was the vehicle that made my life possible, and if I wasn't feeling 100%, I felt like I was failing at being "in control."

The idea of pregnancy was always intimidating because it seemed like the ultimate *loss* of control. I’d look at women with toddlers at the park and see the fraying edges of their clothes, the bags under their eyes, the way their posture had shifted. It looked like hard work. I worried that my body, which I’d spent so long keeping "tight" and efficient, would just break down under that kind of stress. 

But beneath that, I think there was a deeper, quiet fear that I didn't actually have the softness required to be a mother. I was so busy being "the girl who has it together" that I didn't leave much room for the vulnerability of actually needing someone. And now, being pregnant and totally alone? It’s like my worst fear about being overwhelmed has come true, but I’m still expected to be the one who’s "together." It’s an exhausting act to keep up.

In the time before you became pregnant, was there any one person — professional or otherwise — who felt like a consistent, reliable source of support or guidance, someone you could come back to with questions?

To be honest, no. Not really. I’ve always been the one people go to when *they* need something—the steady one, the one who’s got her grades up, the one who doesn't cause drama. I grew up thinking that asking for guidance was a sign of weakness, or at least a sign that you weren't managing your own affairs. If I had a question, I’d just find the answer myself. That’s how I handled school, and that’s how I’ve handled jobs. 

My parents were always around, but the relationship has always been more about performance than vulnerability. They wanted to see me succeed, so I showed them the successes. I didn't show them the parts of me that were questioning things or the parts that felt lost. I think that’s why this pregnancy feels so isolating; I’ve spent twenty-one years training myself to be an island, and now that I’m actually surrounded by an ocean, I don't know how to signal for help.

There wasn't a mentor or a doctor I felt I could sit down with and just talk. Everyone has always seemed so busy, and I didn't want to be the one taking up their time. It’s hard to admit, but I think I’ve been waiting for someone to notice I needed support without me having to ask for it. But that never really happens, does it? People just see what you show them.
What made that person feel reliable or trustworthy as a source of support at that stage?

I think I’m realizing that I’ve never actually *had* that person. I’ve lived my whole life waiting for someone—a mentor, a teacher, maybe a parent—to look at me and say, "I see you're struggling, and I'm here to help." That would have made them trustworthy. But instead, everyone has always been so focused on my "independence" that they never bothered to look past the surface of how "together" I seemed.

When I look back, the people I thought were reliable were really just the ones who rewarded me for being self-sufficient. My teachers loved that I didn't cause trouble, and my parents loved that I didn't need them to bail me out of anything. I mistook their *approval* for *support*. It’s a subtle but massive difference, right? Support is someone being there when you're failing; approval is someone cheering only when you’re winning.

Because I never had someone to sit with during the hard parts, I never developed that muscle. I didn't learn how to lean. Now, in the middle of this pregnancy, when I’m scared or overwhelmed, I keep looking for that "reliable person" in the faces of my midwives or the people at the clinic, but it’s just not there. They’re professional, sure, but they’re just another version of the people who are only interested in my results, not my reality. I’m just waiting for someone to finally be human with me.

As you were thinking about whether to have a baby, did it feel like a decision you were genuinely free to make in your own time and your own way — or were there pressures, expectations, or circumstances that meant it was never quite fully your choice?

That’s a heavy question. To be honest, the word "choice" feels like a luxury I didn’t really have. People talk about "planning" a family like it’s a career move or a vacation, but my life didn't unfold that way. It wasn't a boardroom meeting where I weighed the pros and cons. It was a sudden collision of biology and circumstance that took the reins out of my hands entirely.

I think there’s this unspoken pressure in society—especially in a town like Holden—to be "together," to have your life in neat little rows. When I got pregnant, all those expectations rushed in to fill the silence. There was the pressure to act like I had it all figured out, the pressure to not be "the statistic," and the pressure to just handle it quietly. Even if I wanted to stop and take a breath, the world around me kept moving, expecting me to keep pace. 

I don't think I was genuinely "free" in the way people talk about autonomy. I was just reacting to a situation that was bigger than me, trying to keep my head above water. It feels like my life has been one long performance of independence, and this pregnancy is just the hardest, loneliest act I’ve ever had to pull off. It’s my life, yes, but it doesn’t always feel like it was my *plan*.
Were there financial, relationship, or cultural factors that made you feel like the timing was out of your hands?

They say your twenties are for finding yourself, but mine feel like a relentless race against a clock I didn't set. Financial reality is probably the biggest anchor. I’m working full-time, and every single paycheck is already spoken for before it even hits my account. When you're twenty-one and working entry-level jobs, you don't have "timing." You just have the next rent payment and the fear that if you trip up even once, the whole house of cards collapses.

Then there’s the cultural weight of being here, in a place like Holden. People don’t say it, but you can *feel* it—the expectation that you’re supposed to have a house, a partner, and a plan before you bring a child into the mix. I feel like I’m living proof of the "wrong" way to do things, so I work twice as hard to look like I’m handling it perfectly. I don't want to be the cautionary tale.

If I’d had a partner who was actually there, maybe the timing would’ve felt like a choice. But being solo? It feels like I’m just trying to survive an avalanche. I didn’t choose the timing, and I didn't choose the loneliness. I’m just trying to prove that I’m tough enough to survive it without asking for help, because in my world, asking for help feels like admitting you shouldn't have been in the race to begin with.
Once you were pregnant, how much did you feel like you were in the driving seat of your own care — and were there moments when you felt yourself handing that over to others, willingly or not?

I’d say I’m in the driver’s seat, but the car is on a track I didn't lay down. Every appointment is a series of hoops I jump through. I prepare, I read the charts, I bring my list of questions—that’s me driving. But then I get into that room, and the moment the midwife takes over, the dynamic shifts. It’s like I’m sitting in the car, but she’s the one steering, and I’m just supposed to look out the window and enjoy the ride.

There are definitely moments where I willingly hand over the wheel. When the anxiety gets so loud that I can’t hear myself think, I *want* to give that control to someone else. I want to look at them and say, "Just tell me what to do so I don’t mess this up." I surrender because I’m exhausted by the mental load of being the only one responsible for two lives. 

But then there are the moments where I’m *forced* to hand it over, and that feels different. It’s like when I’m told "everything looks perfect" and my deeper concerns are brushed aside. In those moments, I feel invisible, like my agency has been stripped away by their clinical confidence. I’m the one who has to live with the consequences of this pregnancy, the one who has to go home to the silence, but they’re the ones deciding what’s "important" enough to talk about. It’s a strange, helpless kind of surrender.
Were there points where relying on others felt like a relief, and others where it felt like losing something?

It’s a strange, jagged feeling. Sometimes, when I’m at the clinic, having the midwife tell me exactly what to expect, or having the technician point out the baby’s heartbeat on the screen, it’s a massive relief. It’s a moment where I can stop being the researcher, stop being the one who’s "together," and just let someone else hold the map for a second. It feels like someone else is carrying the weight of the "what-ifs," and for five minutes, I get to just sit there and be taken care of.

But that relief has a sharp edge to it. Because when I lean on them, I feel like I’m losing the one thing I’ve been guarding so fiercely: my identity as someone who can handle this on her own. I feel like I’m admitting that I’m not enough, or that I’m the "young, overwhelmed girl" they probably think I am anyway. It’s like, by accepting their help, I’m confirming their biases. 

It feels like losing a piece of myself, too. I’ve spent twenty-one years curating this version of me who doesn't need a crutch, and every time I have to rely on someone—a doctor who doesn't know my name, or a system that doesn't care about my loneliness—I feel a little bit more hollowed out. I want to be able to lean on someone without feeling like I’m losing my footing entirely. I want support that makes me feel stronger, not support that makes me feel like I’m failing.

When decisions came up during your pregnancy — about tests, appointments, birth plans, or anything else — how did you and your partner navigate those together, and did it ever feel like you weren't quite on the same page?

I think I've mentioned it before, but that "we" just doesn't exist for me. When it comes to birth plans or testing, it’s just me, sitting at my small kitchen table, staring at a printout I got from the clinic. There is no one to have a conversation with, no one to be on or off the same page with. That’s the most isolating part—the finality of it. Every decision I make is entirely my own, and that’s a heavy, crushing realization when you’re twenty-one.

Sometimes, when I’m filling out those forms or thinking about the delivery, I find myself "playing" the conversation in my head. I imagine what a partner would say—maybe they'd tell me to skip a test because it's too much stress, or maybe they'd say, "Let’s do it all so we can be sure." But then I snap back to reality and I’m just staring at a blank space on a sheet of paper. 

It never feels like a "navigation." It feels like a solo climb. I don't have the luxury of checking in with someone else's opinion. It’s just my judgment, my anxiety, and my responsibility. If I’m on a different page than the people around me—or even just different from what I *thought* my life would look like—it’s just another piece of the silence I have to get used to living with. It’s hard to reconcile that with the idea of a "shared" experience.
Were there decisions where your partner's view really mattered to you, and ones where you needed it to be your call alone?

It’s strange to imagine someone else’s view mattering now, because I’ve had to become so self-contained. But if I’m honest? I think I would have valued a partner’s perspective on the "human" stuff—the things that don’t show up on a chart. Like, when to start telling people at work, or how to handle the anxiety of a rough night, or even just deciding on a name. Those are the moments where I feel that sharp, cold ache of being the only one. Having a partner to say, "Hey, we'll figure this out," would have made the world feel a lot less big and scary.

But for the medical decisions—the tests, the scans, the procedures—I think I would have always insisted on being the one to decide. Because I’m the one who has to live in this body, and I’m the one who has to face the results in that quiet room after the appointment ends. I’ve become so protective of my own agency that I almost fear someone else’s input there. I’d be terrified they’d steer me wrong or, worse, that they wouldn't take the medical risks as seriously as I do.

So, it’s a paradox. I crave the support for the emotional weight, but I’ve built a fortress around the clinical side because I don't trust anyone else to care as much as I do. It’s a lonely way to live, but at least I know the decisions are mine, for better or for worse.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

You know, there’s one thing I keep thinking about—the way people talk about "birth plans." Everywhere I look, there’s this assumption that you’re going to show up to the hospital with a partner who’s read the book, has the snacks packed, and knows exactly how to advocate for you when you can't speak for yourself. 

I don’t have that. My birth plan isn't about music choices or lighting; it’s about survival. It’s about being twenty-one and walking into that room knowing I have to be my own voice, my own shield, and my own anchor all at the same time. I wish the medical system wasn't so built around the "couple" model. I wish there was a way to signal that you’re doing this alone so that a nurse, or a midwife, or *someone* could just acknowledge that and say, "We’ve got you." 

I don't need pity, and I don't need someone to do it for me. I just need to feel seen as a whole person, not just a low-risk chart that’s easy to ignore. Because the silence of being alone is the hardest labor I’m going to have to get through, and I’m terrified that when I’m in the middle of it, I’ll be the only one who remembers I’m a human being, not just a patient. Thank you for listening. It... it actually feels a little lighter to have said that out loud.
